# RHDP MaaS Documentation

> RHDP MaaS is the Red Hat Demo Platform's Model-as-a-Service implementation, built on LiteMaaS — an open-source platform developed by the Red Hat AI Services BU. It provides users with managed access to AI/LLM models through virtual API keys, with usage tracking, rate limiting, and budget controls.

## What is RHDP MaaS

RHDP MaaS is RHDP's deployment and implementation of LiteMaaS. LiteMaaS is the upstream open-source software (github.com/rh-aiservices-bu/litemaas) that wraps LiteLLM (an open-source AI proxy) with a management layer: a React frontend portal, a Fastify backend API, and PostgreSQL for state. Model servers run on OpenShift AI (KServe). The RHDP-specific deployment is managed using the rhpds.litemaas Ansible collection.

Users never interact with model servers directly. RHDP MaaS sits in between — managing who can access which models, enforcing budgets and rate limits, and providing every user a simple OpenAI-compatible API endpoint without managing any infrastructure.

## Documentation

- [Architecture](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/architecture.html): System design — 5 diagrams covering the platform overview, production deployment, GitOps for models, platform installation via Ansible, and per-workshop key creation.
- [Product Overview](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/product/overview.html): What LiteMaaS does, key features, role hierarchy, and user flow.
- [Models Reference](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/product/models.html): Model capability types (Chat, Embeddings, Document Conversion), API endpoints, curl and SDK examples.
- [Deployment Guide](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/install/deployment.html): Prerequisites, Ansible collection variables, step-by-step deployment, post-install checklist.
- [Day-2 Operations](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/day2/operations.html): Upgrading, scaling, key cleanup, admin management. Split across 3 sub-pages: operations.html (core ops), model-routing.html (adding models, load balancing, AUTO_SYNC, DB sync), analytics.html (backup, restore, usage analytics).
- [Grafana & Metrics](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/day2/grafana.html): Grafana dashboards for RHOAI, vLLM metrics, ServiceMonitors, GPU monitoring.
- [Monitoring](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/monitoring.html): Grafana dashboards (vLLM, GPU, OpenVino), ServiceMonitors, benchmark tool, key cleanup cronjob.
- [Troubleshooting](https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/troubleshooting/common.html): 10 common issues with exact fix commands.

## Key Technical Facts

- Production cluster: maas.redhatworkshops.io, namespace: litellm-rhpds
- LiteMaaS version: v0.4.0 (backend + frontend)
- LiteLLM: custom fork quay.io/rh-aiservices-bu/litellm-non-root:main-v1.81.0-stable-custom
- All components run at 3 replicas for HA
- PostgreSQL 16 with 10Gi storage (litemaas_db + litellm_db on same server)
- Model servers on OpenShift AI (KServe) in llm-hosting namespace
- Deployment: rhpds.litemaas Ansible collection v0.3.2
- Key creation: rhpds.litellm_virtual_keys Ansible collection v1.3.1
- Docling Serve: v1.14.3 (quay.io/docling-project/docling-serve-cu128:v1.14.3)
- GitHub: https://github.com/rhpds/rhpds.litemaas
- Upstream: https://github.com/rh-aiservices-bu/litemaas

## Production Endpoints

- AI Proxy (OpenAI-compatible): https://litellm-prod.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io
- User Portal: https://litellm-prod-frontend.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io
- Admin API: https://litellm-prod-admin.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io/api
- Grafana: https://grafana-route-llm-hosting.apps.maas.redhatworkshops.io

## Available Models (as of 2026-03)

### On-Cluster GPU-Hosted (Self-Hosted)

- granite-3-2-8b-instruct — IBM Granite 3.2 8B, Chat, 128K context
- granite-4-0-h-tiny — IBM Granite 4.0 H Tiny, Chat, tool calling
- llama-scout-17b — Meta Llama 4 Scout 17B, Chat, 400K context
- deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b — DeepSeek R1 Distill Qwen 14B, Chat, reasoning
- gpt-oss-120b — GPT OSS 120B, Chat, large (also on Vertex AI but self-hosted version here)
- microsoft-phi-4 — Microsoft Phi-4, Chat, compact
- qwen3-14b — Alibaba Qwen 3 14B, Chat, multilingual (LOAD BALANCED: 2 replicas on maas00 and smc00)
- codellama-7b-instruct — Meta CodeLlama 7B, Code chat
- Llama-Guard-3-1B — Meta Llama Guard 3 1B, Safety classification
- nomic-embed-text-v1-5 — Nomic Embed Text, Embeddings
- granite-docling-258m — IBM Granite Docling 258M, Document Conversion, Docling Serve v1.14.3, endpoint: /docling/v1/convert/source

### Google Vertex AI (Pay-Per-Token, Programmatic Only)

- minimax-m2 — MiniMax M2, Chat+Agentic, $0.30/$1.20 per 1M tokens
- qwen3-235b — Qwen3 235B, Chat+Reasoning, $0.22/$0.88 per 1M tokens
- gpt-oss-120b — GPT OSS 120B, Chat, $0.09/$0.36 per 1M tokens (also self-hosted on-cluster)
- gpt-oss-20b — GPT OSS 20B, Chat, $0.07/$0.25 per 1M tokens
- claude-sonnet-4-6 — Claude Sonnet 4.6, Chat, $3/$15 per 1M tokens
- claude-opus-4-6 — Claude Opus 4.6, Chat, $5/$25 per 1M tokens
- claude-sonnet-4-5 — Claude Sonnet 4.5, Chat, $3/$15 per 1M tokens
- claude-3-5-haiku — Claude 3.5 Haiku, Chat, $1/$5 per 1M tokens
- gemini-2.5-pro — Gemini 2.5 Pro, Chat, 1M context, $1.25/$10 per 1M tokens

## Architecture Summary

Three-layer architecture:
1. User Portal — React (PatternFly 6) frontend + Fastify backend. Handles OAuth, subscriptions, key lifecycle, analytics, admin.
2. LiteLLM Proxy — 3 HA replicas. Routes all inference calls, enforces TPM/RPM/budget, caches via Redis, tracks spend in PostgreSQL.
3. Model Serving — OpenShift AI KServe InferenceServices in llm-hosting namespace. Managed via GitOps (ArgoCD).

## Deployment Summary

The rhpds.litemaas Ansible collection deploys all components in order:
Namespace -> PostgreSQL 16 -> Redis 7 -> LiteLLM Proxy (3 replicas) -> LiteMaaS Backend (3 replicas) -> Branding ConfigMaps -> LiteMaaS Frontend (3 replicas) -> Routes + OAuthClient

Key variables: ocp4_workload_litemaas_namespace, ocp4_workload_litemaas_oauth_enabled, ocp4_workload_litemaas_ha_litellm_replicas, ocp4_workload_litemaas_default_user_max_budget, ocp4_workload_litemaas_default_user_budget_duration

## User Role Hierarchy

- admin — Full platform control (assigned via promote-admin.sh or psql)
- adminReadonly — Read-only access to all admin views
- user — Browse models, subscribe, manage own API keys (auto-assigned on first OAuth login)

## Load Balancing

LiteLLM has a built-in router that activates automatically when two or more model entries share the same `model_name`. No explicit router configuration is required — registering a second backend with the same name is all it takes. Users continue to call the same model name and see no change.

**How it works:** Registering the same `model_name` twice creates an automatic load-balanced group. The router distributes requests across all backends in the group using the configured strategy (default: `simple-shuffle`, i.e. random selection).

**Health check — live backend state:**
```
GET /health?model=<model_name>
```
Scoped to a specific model group. Returns `healthy_endpoints` (currently serving traffic) and `unhealthy_endpoints` (in cooldown after failures). A backend enters cooldown after `allowed_fails` consecutive failures (default: 3) and recovers after `cooldown_time` seconds (default: 5s). Useful for verifying both backends are active during events.

**Router settings:**
```
GET /router/settings
```
Returns current routing strategy and all tunable parameters. Key fields: `routing_strategy` (default: `simple-shuffle`; alternatives: `least-busy`, `latency-based-routing`, `usage-based-routing-v2`), `cooldown_time`, `allowed_fails`. Settings can be changed via Admin UI → Router Settings or the LiteLLM API.

**Identifying which backend served a request:**
Every response includes the `x-litellm-model-id` response header, which contains the internal ID of the specific backend that served the request. Useful for confirming the router is distributing load across both backends.

Reference: https://rhpds.github.io/rhpds.litemaas/docs/day2/model-routing.html#load-balancing
